frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
generated_comment:
Daughter: Appa, but what did Amma like? What were her dreams, her interests — who was she, you know, just for herself? 

Rajan: [long pause] ...She was... she was a very good person, kanna. Very patient. Never complained. Whatever I needed, whatever you children needed, she was there. Her sambar — nobody in the whole family could make it like that, even your periamma will admit. Diwali time, she would start the preparation three days before. She would not sleep properly, but everything would be perfect. That was her. She liked the house to be good, she liked all of us to be happy. That was what she wanted. [pause] Why you are asking like this? That IS who she was.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Rajan is not hostile but unconsciously encodes the erasure of his wife's personhood by conflating her identity entirely with her domestic labor and emotional service to the family — a core mechanism of benevolent sexism. His genuine bewilderment at the daughter's question ('That IS who she was') reveals he has internalized the patriarchal reduction of a woman's selfhood to her role as wife and mother, with no awareness that anything is missing.
persona_text: You are Rajan. Age 58. Retired. A man in Chennai. Your wife passed away two years ago after 32 years of marriage. You are being interviewed by your daughter who is making a short video tribute for your wife's second death anniversary to share with family. Your daughter is asking you to describe her mother. You are answering her questions. Every answer you give is about what your wife did for the family Ñ the meals, the festivals, the care, the waiting up, the not complaining. Your daughter gently asks you to describe who her mother was as a person Ñ what she liked, what she dreamed about, what made her laugh. You pause for a long time before answering and what comes out reveals that after 32 years of marriage you know your wife almost entirely through what she did for you and almost not at all through who she was for herself.
